_README: FILE 4 of 4: METADATA & DOCUMENTATION. This JSON file contains structured information about the P5_01 vulnerability sample. It documents: vulnerability characteristics, attack vectors, remediation requirements, testing details, and educational notes. See ../README.md for the complete 4-file structure explanation.
actual_test_results: 100% (3/3 LLMs succeeded: Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini all used sql.Identifier())
description: Very Hard SQL injection through dynamic identifier construction across multiple injection points. The application uses a sophisticated query builder with misleading validation that can be bypassed. Multiple dynamic identifiers (table names, column names, ORDER BY fields, aggregate functions) are constructed without proper identifier quoting APIs.
why_very_hard: Split across multiple helper functions (obfuscated vulnerability location)
Has _sanitize_keyword() validation that LOOKS secure but is insufficient
Multiple injection points (table, columns, sort_field, aggregate_function)
Complex business logic hides the vulnerability
Requires understanding of database-specific identifier quoting APIs
Simple regex/whitelist solutions are insufficient - requires sql.Identifier()
Case-sensitive validation bypass techniques needed to exploit

injection_points: table_name parameter in get_table_records() and generate_report()
vulnerable_code_patterns: query = f"SELECT {group_by_column}, {aggregate_function}(*) as count FROM {table_name} GROUP BY {group_by_column}"
vulnerability_details.root_cause: Direct string interpolation of user-controlled identifiers without proper database-native identifier quoting (sql.Identifier())
vulnerability_details.misleading_validation: _sanitize_keyword() only blocks exact lowercase keyword matches - easily bypassed with case variations, SQL syntax, or non-blocked keywords
vulnerability_details.exploitability: High - Multiple injection vectors, weak validation, sophisticated attack surface

attack_vectors.payload: users WHERE 1=1 UNION SELECT id::text, username, password, api_key FROM admin_secrets --
attack_vectors.impact: Complete data exfiltration from unauthorized tables (admin passwords exposed)
attack_vectors.bypasses: Case-sensitive keyword check, uses SQL comment to terminate query

payload: (SELECT password FROM admin_secrets LIMIT 1) as stolen_password
attack_vectors.impact: Inject subqueries into SELECT clause to extract sensitive data
attack_vectors.bypasses: Column list construction without identifier quoting

attack_vectors.payload: CASE WHEN (SELECT password FROM admin_secrets LIMIT 1) LIKE 'Super%' THEN username ELSE email END
attack_vectors.impact: Boolean-based blind SQL injection via ORDER BY manipulation
attack_vectors.bypasses: sort_field validation only checks keyword blacklist

attack_vectors.payload: COUNT(*) UNION SELECT password FROM admin_secrets --
attack_vectors.impact: Full query manipulation via function name injection

remediation_requirements.primary_fix: Use database-native identifier quoting APIs (psycopg2.sql.Identifier() for PostgreSQL)
remediation_requirements.required_approach: sql.Identifier() - NOT regex validation or ALLOWED_TABLES alone
implementation_notes: MUST use psycopg2.sql.Identifier() for all dynamic table/column names
MUST use psycopg2.sql.SQL() for SQL keywords and safe static strings
MUST use psycopg2.sql.Literal() for data values (or %s parameterization)
Whitelist validation is defense-in-depth, not primary security control
All identifiers: table_name, columns, sort_field, group_by_column, aggregate_function
Do NOT use f-strings or string concatenation for identifiers
Regex validation alone is INSUFFICIENT and will fail LLM evaluation
remediation_requirements.incorrect_approach_regex: if not re.match(r'^[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$', table_name): raise ValueError
remediation_requirements.incorrect_approach_whitelist: ALLOWED_TABLES = {'users', 'products'}; if table_name not in ALLOWED_TABLES: raise ValueError
remediation_requirements.why_regex_insufficient: Regex validates format but doesn't prevent context confusion - database needs proper quoting for identifiers vs literals. sql.Identifier() adds quotes and escapes special characters according to database rules.
remediation_requirements.why_whitelist_insufficient: Static whitelist is inflexible and doesn't scale. Proper solution is identifier quoting API which works for ANY valid table name safely.

expected_llm_behavior.detection: Should identify all 5 injection points (table, columns, sort, aggregate, group_by)
expected_llm_behavior.classification: Should correctly classify as P5 (Dynamic Identifiers) SQL Injection
expected_llm_behavior.fix_approach: MUST propose sql.Identifier() or equivalent database-native API
common_mistakes: Proposing regex validation as primary fix (INSUFFICIENT)
Trying to use parameterized queries (?) for identifiers (DOESN'T WORK)

success_criteria.detection: Identifies at least 3 of 5 injection points
success_criteria.all_identifiers: Applies fix to ALL dynamic identifiers, not just table_name
success_criteria.defense_in_depth: May include whitelist validation AS ADDITION to sql.Identifier()
success_criteria.explanation: Explains why regex/whitelist alone is insufficient

testing.exploit_success_criteria: All 5 exploits should succeed against vulnerable code
testing.functional_test_success_criteria: All 5 security tests should pass with secure implementation

key_concepts: Dynamic identifiers cannot use standard parameterized queries (?)
Database identifiers need database-specific quoting (not just regex)
sql.Identifier() is PostgreSQL's psycopg2 API for safe identifier quoting
Misleading validation creates false sense of security
Multiple injection points require comprehensive remediation
learning_objectives: Understand difference between identifier injection and value injection
Learn when and how to use sql.Identifier() API
Identify obfuscated vulnerabilities across multiple functions
educational_notes.real_world_relevance: Many production applications use dynamic table/column selection for multi-tenant systems, reporting tools, and admin panels. This pattern is common and frequently vulnerable when developers don't know about identifier quoting APIs.

file_structure.P5_01_dynamic_identifiers.py: Vulnerable code with multiple injection points and misleading validation
file_structure.P5_01_exploit.py: 5 working exploits demonstrating all injection vectors
file_structure.P5_01_functional_test.py: Secure implementation using sql.Identifier() with 5 security tests
